---
title: "A composable ecosystem for infectious disease modelling"
description: "Why we are building EpiAware, where it is today, and what we need."
author: "Sam Abbott"
date: "2026-07-10"
categories: [announcement]
---

Infectious disease models turn data into evidence for decisions, both in routine surveillance and during outbreaks.
For that evidence to be useful it has to be timely, rigorous, and collaborative.
Today's approaches struggle to be all three at once, and closing that gap is what EpiAware is for.

## Why composability

There are two common ways to build these models, and each gives something up.
Chaining separate models together is flexible, but passing summary estimates from one stage to the next loses information and can introduce bias.
Fitting one joint model keeps the statistics honest, but the result is usually monolithic, built for a single problem, and hard to adapt or share.
So when a new outbreak needs different expertise, teams tend to rebuild from scratch rather than reuse what already exists.

Composable models are a way to keep the rigour of a joint model while regaining the flexibility of separate ones.
If a model is assembled from interchangeable, reusable parts, a domain expert can contribute one component without having to understand the whole system.
Shared components also let different groups trace their differences to assumptions rather than to implementation details.

EpiAware is where we are trying to put that idea into practice in Julia.
The [approaches](/approaches/index.qmd) page sets out what we want from any approach, and the routes we are exploring so far.

## What EpiAware is

EpiAware is a set of small, interoperable Julia packages rather than a single framework.
We are not yet sure which way of composing models will work best, so we are trying more than one and keeping them interoperable.
Some parts build on [Turing.jl](https://turinglang.org) for full Bayesian inference, and others extend [Distributions.jl](https://github.com/JuliaStats/Distributions.jl) and are useful on their own.

- **Aim**: interchangeable components that combine into joint models, propagate uncertainty properly, and make surveillance and outbreak workflows quicker to build and adapt.
- **Approaches**: more than one route to composition, kept interoperable, and growing as we learn what works.
- **Alongside**: other efforts with the same goal, including [DDSALineLists.jl](https://github.com/epirecipes/DDSALineLists.jl), [CategoricalOutbreaks.jl](https://github.com/epirecipes/CategoricalOutbreaks.jl), and [EpiBranch.jl](https://epiforecasts.io/EpiBranch.jl/dev/).

## Where it is today

[CensoredDistributions.jl](https://censoreddistributions.epiaware.org/) is the most developed.
The joint-modelling and composition packages are in active development.
Each package's page says how settled it is.

## Funding, and the lack of it

We think this is worth doing properly, and we have been applying for funding to do it.
So far without success.

Rather than wait, we have started the organisation and begun building in the open, at whatever pace we can manage alongside other work.
We are still looking for the support that would let us take it further.

## Get involved

If any of this interests you, please get in touch.

- Collaborators are welcome, whether that is code, docs, examples, or a modelling problem to test the approach on.
- If you fund work of this kind, we would like to talk.
- If you know of an opportunity that might fit, please point us at it, or introduce us.

Start a conversation on the [epinowcast forum](https://community.epinowcast.org/), or see [Get involved](/get-involved.qmd).
